START.label: Begin
START.text: Good evening. Take a breath. Let's look at your day — not to judge it, but to understand it.

A1_OPEN.label: Day in a word
A1_OPEN.text: How would you describe today in one word?

options.text: Productive
Tough
Mixed
Frustrating

A1_Q2_HIGH.label: What worked?
A1_Q2_HIGH.text: You called it '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. When something went well today, what made it happen?

options.text: I prepared and executed deliberately
I adapted when the plan changed
The team pulled together
Things just fell into place

A1_Q2_LOW.label: What broke?
A1_Q2_LOW.text: You called it '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. When things got hard, what was your first instinct?

options.text: Find what I could still control
Push through regardless
Feel frustrated by the circumstances
Wait for someone to step in

A1_Q3.label: Losing control
A1_Q3.text: Think about the moment today when you had the least control. What did you do with that feeling?

options.text: Acknowledged it, then focused on what I could still change
Used it as fuel to push harder
Let it color the rest of my day
Buried it and hoped it would pass

A1_R_INTERNAL.label: Victor
A1_R_INTERNAL.text: You navigated today rather than just surviving it. That's not about being in control of everything — it's about staying in contact with what you can affect. That instinct is worth noticing.

A1_R_EXTERNAL.label: Victim
A1_R_EXTERNAL.text: Today pulled your attention outward — to what others did or didn't do, to things you couldn't change. That's a natural response to a hard day. But somewhere in that stretch, you made calls. What was the smallest one you made entirely on your own?

BRIDGE_1_2.label: Axis I → II
BRIDGE_1_2.text: Now let's shift — from how you handled what happened, to what you gave while it was happening.

A2_OPEN.label: How I showed up
A2_OPEN.text: Think about the people you interacted with today. Which best describes how you showed up?

options.text: I actively helped someone who needed it
I shared knowledge or taught someone
I focused mostly on getting my own work done
I felt let down by how others treated my effort

values: I actively helped someone who needed it
I shared knowledge or taught someone

A2_Q2_CONTRIB.label: Why I gave
A2_Q2_CONTRIB.text: When you gave today — time, attention, effort — what prompted it?

options.text: I saw a need and had the capacity to fill it
I've been on the receiving end — it costs little
I was asked and I said yes
I hoped it would come back to me somehow

A2_Q2_ENTITLE.label: No credit
A2_Q2_ENTITLE.text: When your effort wasn't acknowledged today, what happened inside you?

options.text: Let it go — the work matters more than credit
Noted it, but kept going anyway
Felt quietly resentful
Started holding back my effort

A2_Q3.label: Without credit
A2_Q3.text: If tomorrow no one noticed anything you did today — same work, zero recognition — would you have shown up any differently?

options.text: No — the work is the point
Probably not, though recognition still matters to me
Honestly yes — I need to feel seen to keep going
Yes — I'm exhausted by giving without return

A2_R_CONTRIBUTION.label: Giver
A2_R_CONTRIBUTION.text: You gave today without needing a receipt. That's not a small thing — most people are quietly keeping score. The fact that you're not, or at least trying not to, is what separates someone who grows an organization from someone who merely inhabits it.

A2_R_ENTITLEMENT.label: Entitled
A2_R_ENTITLEMENT.text: Wanting recognition isn't a flaw — it's human. But notice what it does: when the ledger becomes the point, every interaction becomes a transaction. What would it feel like to give something today that you'll never get credit for, and find that enough?

BRIDGE_2_3.label: Axis II → III
BRIDGE_2_3.text: One more shift — from what you contributed, to who was in your field of vision when you did it.

A3_OPEN.label: Who I saw
A3_OPEN.text: When you think about today's hardest moment, whose experience beyond your own comes to mind?

options.text: Just mine — it was my problem to navigate
My team — we were all under the same pressure
A colleague who had it harder than me
The person we're ultimately trying to serve

A3_Q2_WIDE.label: With others
A3_Q2_WIDE.text: You held others in view today. When you noticed someone struggling, what did you do?

options.text: I checked in — even when I had my own fires
I felt their pressure and wanted to help
I noticed but kept moving — I had to prioritize
I tried to help but my own needs won out

A3_Q2_NARROW.label: Just me
A3_Q2_NARROW.text: Your focus was mostly on your own experience today. What pulls you out of that frame when it does shift?

options.text: When I'm less overwhelmed and have more bandwidth
When someone directly asks me for help
When I remember what the team is trying to build together
When I see someone visibly struggling beside me

A3_Q3.label: What mattered
A3_Q3.text: At the end of today, what felt most meaningful — even briefly?

options.text: A result I personally delivered
A moment I made easier for someone else
A decision that will matter for the team, not just me
Nothing — I just got through it

A3_R_ALTROCENTRIC.label: Altrocentric
A3_R_ALTROCENTRIC.text: You moved through today with others in your field of vision. That's not a soft skill — it's the clearest signal of someone who creates value beyond what their job description requires. The widest contributors are rarely the loudest ones. They're the ones who know what the room needs before the room says it.

A3_R_SELFCENTRIC.label: Self-centric
A3_R_SELFCENTRIC.text: Today's frame was mostly your own. That's honest, and often necessary. But Maslow's last and least-cited insight was this: the people who reported the deepest meaning weren't the ones who had the most — they were the ones who had shifted from 'what do I need?' to 'what does this moment need from me?' What would that shift feel like tomorrow?

SUMMARY.label: Today's map
SUMMARY.text:
You described today as '{A1_OPEN.answer}'.

On Agency: you leaned toward {axis1_dominant} — {axis1_summary}.

On Contribution: you leaned toward {axis2_dominant} — {axis2_summary}.

On Radius: you leaned toward {axis3_dominant} — {axis3_summary}.

These aren't grades. They're coordinates. Same time tomorrow.
